If you select the 1920x1080 resolution, you can then select the frame rate that you want to capture the video at.

The frame rate you select will depend on the specific requirements of your project and the type of video you are working with.

There are several frame rates available in OBS, including:

* "Default": This frame rate is the default setting and is suitable for most scenarios.
* "High Performance": This frame rate is designed to capture video at the highest possible frame rate with minimal latency. This frame rate can be useful when working with fast-moving video or for gaming.
* "High Quality": This frame rate is designed to capture video at the highest possible quality with minimal compression. This frame rate can be useful when working with high-quality video or for professional video production.
* "Indistinguishable Lossless": This frame rate is designed to capture video at the highest possible quality with minimal compression, this frame rate is designed to keep the video as close to the original quality as possible.

You can choose the frame rate that best fits your needs, but in general, the "High Quality" frame rate is recommended when working with VHS tapes, as it will capture the video at the highest possible quality with minimal compression.

As Jacob and Shiva approach the offering site, Jacob hesitates and says, "I know it seems strange to give up our food, but Lorelei specifically requested it."

Shiva scowls and looks up the mountain, then says, "I understand. But it seems cruel to offer food when your village is already suffering from lack of it. Are you sure this is what Lorelei wants?"

Jacob nods hesitantly. "Yes, I heard it myself on the mountain. Lorelei wants an offering of food to appease Eri and Racita."

Shiva sighs and reluctantly adds his offerings to the pile. "Well, if this is what she wants, I suppose we should oblige. But I can't shake the feeling that there has to be another way."

The terms "stash" and "repository" are often used in the context of software development, but they can also be used in other contexts outside of software development.

For example, "stash" can be used to refer to a secret store of goods or valuables, hidden away for safekeeping. In this context, a "stash" might refer to a hidden cache of money, drugs, or other illicit items.

Similarly, "repository" can be used in other contexts to refer to a place where something is stored or collected. For example, a museum might have a repository of artifacts that are not currently on display, but are being preserved for future generations. In the context of academic research, a repository might refer to a digital archive of scholarly articles and other publications.

In general, the terms "stash" and "repository" can be used in any context where there is a need to store or collect items in a central location, whether that be physical goods, digital files, or other types of assets.
